title: ISIS Crackdown, Sanctions Evasion Bust, and Stalled Peace Process Mark a Turbulent Day
subhead: Turkey arrested 110 suspected ISIS operatives, a German probe exposed a €30M sanctions-evasion network running through Turkish intermediaries, and the PKK peace process ground to a near-standstill — while Ankara navigates the Iran war's economic fallout and an emerging regional power bloc.

sections.heading: Major ISIS Operation: 110 Arrested Across Three Provinces
sections.body:
Turkish counter-terror police arrested 110 people on suspicion of ISIS ties in simultaneous raids across Istanbul and two other provinces on Tuesday. The operation, coordinated by the Istanbul chief prosecutor's office, targeted suspects accused of organizing illegal classes, indoctrinating children with ISIS ideology, collecting funds, and recruiting members. Police seized four rifles, 90 cartridges, banned publications, organizational documents, and digital materials.

The sweep comes amid a sharp escalation in ISIS activity on Turkish soil. Last week, authorities detained 324 people in raids across 47 provinces. On April 7, a gunman opened fire outside the Israeli consulate in Istanbul; the interior minister linked one suspect to an organization that exploits religion, which Turkish media identified as ISIS. In December, ISIS militants killed three police officers in Yalova, prompting Turkey to round up over 600 suspected members in the following weeks.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is facing a sustained ISIS resurgence at home even as regional attention focuses on the Iran war — and the pace of operations suggests the threat is intensifying, not diminishing.

sections.heading: German Probe Exposes €30M Russia Sanctions Network via Turkey
sections.body:
A four-year German investigation has exposed a Lübeck-based trading firm, Global Trade, that allegedly moved roughly 16,000 shipments worth over €30 million in dual-use components to Russia through Turkish intermediaries, according to case files reviewed by BILD and POLITICO. The firm's managing director, Nikita S., was arrested at the Radisson Blu in Lübeck on the morning of the raid; investigators describe him as the binding link between the German company and its Russian controllers.

At the top of the network sat Kolovrat/Siderius, a Russian company sanctioned by the U.S. for operating in Russia's manufacturing sector. German investigators allege it functioned as the operational core of a procurement system supplying Russian defense-linked entities. After sanctions tightened post-invasion, Global Trade pivoted from direct exports to routing shipments through third countries — often Turkey. A Turkish company, MR Global, served as the formal consignee while acting as a transit hub. German front companies — ER Industriebedarf GmbH and Amtech Solutions — further obscured the chain. Internal communications were blunt: "Make it look clean. No Russian reference anywhere"; "Remove all documents from the boxes before shipment"; "We need a different consignee — Turkey works."
sections.whyItMatters: The case exposes a persistent structural weakness in Western sanctions enforcement — and puts Turkey's role as a transit point for sanctioned goods back under the microscope at a moment when Ankara is trying to position itself as a credible diplomatic actor.

sections.heading: PKK Peace Process Stalls as Iran War Complicates Calculus
sections.body:
Turkey's peace process with the Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK) has ground to a near-standstill, with both sides effectively waiting for the other to make the next move. Just two weeks after parliament issued recommendations on advancing the process, the Iran war broke out, injecting fresh instability into an already fragile dynamic. The pro-Kurdish DEM Party issued one of its strongest criticisms yet of the government's handling of talks in late April, while a senior militant commander declared the initiative "frozen" by Ankara in early May.

The Foreign Affairs analysis underscores the risk: a destabilized Iran could embolden Kurdish groups across the region, complicating Ankara's efforts to demobilize the PKK. Turkey has not allowed the U.S. or Israel to use its airspace for strikes against Iran, but Iranian ballistic missiles have entered Turkish airspace — four were intercepted by NATO systems, not Turkish air defenses, a consequence of the S-400 purchase that excluded Turkey from critical NATO programs. The PKK issue and the Iran war are now locked together: neither can be resolved without reference to the other.
sections.whyItMatters: The peace process was already fragile before the Iran war; now it's essentially frozen. If the conflict escalates on Turkey's southeastern border, Kurdish militant groups may calculate they have more leverage, not less.

sections.heading: Antalya Forum and the Middle Power Quartet
sections.body:
President Erdoğan used the Antalya Diplomacy Forum to position Turkey as the convener of a new middle-power bloc. He brought together leaders from 150 countries and warned that the global order stands on a "dangerous threshold," calling for diplomacy over conflict. Erdoğan also stated that Ankara was working to extend the Iran ceasefire and continue talks, offering Turkey as a hub for peace negotiations.

Separately, a quartet of Pakistan, Turkey, Egypt, and Saudi Arabia has crystallized into the primary negotiating channel between Tehran and Washington. The group called for an end to Gulf hostilities and, according to The Conversation and Foreign Affairs analysis, may represent the beginning of a new regional order designed to curb both Iranian and Israeli dominance. Each country brings a key asset: Pakistan's nuclear deterrent, Saudi Arabia's oil reserves, Egypt's Suez Canal control, and Turkey's NATO membership. But the bloc is not a natural alliance — Turkey-Saudi relations only thawed in 2022 after the Khashoggi crisis, and Turkey-Egypt rapprochement began in 2025.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is actively building a diplomatic framework that could outlast the Iran war — but the bloc's durability depends on whether these four countries can sustain cooperation when their underlying rivalries resurface.

sections.heading: Central Bank Raises Inflation Forecast to 26%, Holds Rates at 37%
sections.body:
The Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey held its key interest rate at 37% on April 22, but on May 14 it raised its end-2026 official inflation forecast to 26%, up sharply from the 15–21% range projected in February. The bank warned that Iran war fallout could yet change the inflation outlook, and that all policy options remain on the table. Market participants' inflation expectations worsened further in May, with forecasts rising for both year-end and 12-month horizons.

The war's economic toll is becoming clearer. Inbound visitors from four Gulf countries plummeted nearly 80% in March — only 691 Kuwaitis, 606 UAE citizens, 394 Qataris, and 265 Bahraini nationals traveled to Turkey that month, according to Ministry of Culture and Tourism data. Q2 is expected to be significantly tougher than Q1, with cancellations and a shift toward last-minute bookings. Skift reports that the industry is watching whether this is a temporary shock or a structural reset for one of Turkey's most valuable visitor segments.
sections.whyItMatters: The central bank's forecast revision is the clearest quantitative signal yet that the Iran war is biting into Turkey's economy — and the tourism data confirms the damage is not abstract but already visible in booking numbers.

sections.heading: Energy Corridors Accelerate as Hormuz Disruption Reshapes Trade
sections.body:
The Strait of Hormuz closure has turbocharged Turkey's significance as an energy transit route. Iraq has commenced construction of the Basra–Haditha pipeline, with a planned capacity of 2.5 million barrels per day and $1.5 billion allocated, linking southern Iraq's Hormuz-locked fields to outlets at Ceyhan (Turkey), Baniyas (Syria), and Aqaba (Jordan). Iraq has also reportedly awarded a multibillion-dollar contract to Chinese companies under an oil-for-projects framework. IEA chief Fatih Birol called the Hormuz closure the "largest energy security threat the world has ever faced" and has specifically advocated for a new Basra–Ceyhan pipeline to reduce Hormuz reliance.

The existing Kirkuk–Ceyhan pipeline is currently running at approximately 250,000 bpd but could ramp to 400,000–650,000 bpd in the coming months. Meanwhile, the UAE's exit from OPEC — driven in part by the Iran war's impact on its infrastructure — is reshaping Gulf energy politics. Turkey hosted Kuwait's foreign minister as the Gulf rift deepens, forcing Ankara to balance ties between Saudi Arabia and Abu Dhabi at a delicate moment.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's geography just became its most valuable strategic asset. Every new pipeline plan that terminates at Ceyhan reinforces Turkey's role as the primary Hormuz bypass — but that leverage depends on Iraq's political stability and Turkey's ability to navigate competing Gulf interests.

bottomLine: Turkey had a consequential day across security, diplomacy, and economics. The ISIS crackdown reveals a worsening domestic terror threat that exists in parallel to — not beneath — the Iran war. The German sanctions-evasion case puts Turkey's trade transparency back in the spotlight at the worst possible moment. The PKK peace process is effectively frozen, and the Iran war makes it harder, not easier, to unfreeze. On the diplomatic front, Ankara is actively building a four-country bloc with Pakistan, Egypt, and Saudi Arabia — an ambitious play that could reshape regional order if it holds, but the alliances are fragile and recent. Economically, the central bank's revised inflation forecast and the 80% collapse in Gulf tourism confirm the war is no longer an external risk but an active drag. The one clear winner: Turkey's energy transit position, with every new pipeline plan reinforcing Ceyhan as the Hormuz bypass of choice. But that leverage is only as durable as Iraq's politics and Turkey's ability to balance competing Gulf patrons.
sources: Anadolu Agency, Hürriyet Daily News, POLITICO, BILD, Reuters, Skift, Foreign Affairs, The Conversation, Al-Monitor, NextBigFuture, DevDiscourse, OilPrice.com, Forbes, ING Think, Daily Sabah, Japan Times, Kurdistan24, Al Arabiya, TRT World, MSN
